statement: The EEA Agreement covers the EU's single market (four freedoms: goods, services, capital, persons) but explicitly excludes several major policy areas that full EU membership would encompass. Key exclusions: (1) Common Agricultural Policy (CAP) — Iceland maintains its own agricultural subsidies and import protections; (2) Common Fisheries Policy (CFP) — Iceland retains sovereign control over its 200-nautical-mile EEZ and fisheries management; (3) Customs Union — Iceland sets its own external tariffs and can negotiate independent trade agreements; (4) Common Foreign and Security Policy (CFSP) — Iceland has an independent foreign policy, though it often aligns with EU positions; (5) Justice and Home Affairs (JHA) — Iceland participates in parts of Schengen but is not bound by EU criminal law, asylum, or immigration directives; (6) Monetary policy — Iceland maintains its own currency (ISK) and central bank; (7) Taxation — the EU has no competence over Iceland's tax policy through the EEA. Full EU membership would extend EU authority to all these areas, subject to any opt-outs or transition periods negotiated in the accession treaty.
source_name: EEA Agreement, Protocol 47 and Annexes I–XXII; EFTA Secretariat — 'The EEA Agreement: Coverage and Scope'
caveats: The boundary between 'EEA-relevant' and 'excluded' is not always sharp. Some EU legislation in excluded areas (e.g., financial regulation, energy, environment) is incorporated into the EEA if it has single market implications. The EEA's dynamic incorporation mechanism means the boundary shifts as new EU legislation is adopted. In practice, Iceland implements more EU law than the formal exclusions suggest.

related_entries.statement: The EEA Agreement gives Iceland, Liechtenstein, and Norway access to the EU's single market by incorporating relevant EU legislation into their national law. The EEA covers approximately 70–75% of the EU acquis by volume (measured by number of legal acts), focusing on the 'four freedoms': free movement of goods, persons, services, and capital. However, major policy areas are excluded from the EEA: the Common Agricultural Policy (CAP), the Common Fisheries Policy (CFP), the Customs Union (Iceland maintains its own customs tariff), the Common Foreign and Security Policy (CFSP), Justice and Home Affairs (partially — Schengen is separate), Economic and Monetary Union (euro), and the EU's common trade policy (Iceland conducts its own trade agreements with non-EU countries). The EFTA Surveillance Authority (ESA) and EFTA Court provide institutional oversight for EEA EFTA states, mirroring the European Commission and CJEU respectively. As of 2025, over 13,000 EU legal acts had been incorporated into the EEA Agreement since 1994, with approximately 200–400 new acts incorporated annually through EEA Joint Committee decisions.

Iceland implements approximately 75% of EU single market legislation through the EEA Agreement without having any voting rights in the EU institutions that draft and adopt these laws. The EEA Joint Committee, where Iceland has a formal seat, operates by consensus but in practice rubber-stamps EU legislation — Iceland has never exercised its Article 102 'right of reservation' to veto an EEA-relevant act. The EFTA pillar's consultative role is limited to providing input before legislation is finalised, with no amendment power. This arrangement has been described as a 'fax democracy' by Norwegian critics — Iceland receives the laws by fax (metaphorically) and implements them without meaningful influence over their content.

Iceland's participation in the EU single market through the EEA Agreement creates a significant democratic deficit. As of 2025, the EEA Agreement has incorporated approximately 13,000 EU legal acts into Icelandic law. These acts were adopted by the EU Council and European Parliament — institutions in which Iceland has no representation. The EEA Joint Committee incorporates approximately 300–500 new EU acts per year. While the EEA EFTA states have a formal 'decision-shaping' role (participation in expert groups, consultation during drafting), they have no 'decision-making' power — no vote in Council, no MEPs, no Commissioner. The Icelandic Althingi's European Affairs Committee reviews EEA-relevant legislation, but in practice it acts as a rubber stamp: of the ~13,000 incorporated acts, Iceland has never exercised its Article 102 'right of reservation' to veto a single one. Full EU membership would replace this 'legislation without representation' with formal voting rights: a Council vote, 6+ European Parliament seats, and a European Commissioner.

citations.report_title: 25. þáttur Silfursins: Stefnt að kosningu um ESB í sumar

claims.claim_text: Innan ESB er það hvert aðildarríki sem útfærir aflaheimildir sínar og skiptir þeim milli fyrirtækja; ríkið myndi áfram stýra úthlutun og Ísland gæti haldið kvótakerfinu sínu.

citations.report_title: Björn hrekur fullyrðingu um afdrif sjávarauðlinda Íslendinga í ESB

claims.claim_text: Erlend skip geta aðeins veiðt í íslenskri lögsögu ef þau skrá sig undir íslenskan fána samkvæmt íslenskum lögum, og einungis ef íslensk lög leyfa slíkt.
Breytingar á veiðistjórnarkerfið á Íslandi, eins og að allur afli fari á markað og rekjanleiki afla frá sjó til búðar, rúmast innan regluverks ESB.
Það er ósatt að ESB-aðild muni sjálfkrafa hafa í för með sér að hingað komi erlend skip með erlendar áhafnir sem leggi sjávarplássin í eyði.
Sjavarutvegur er undanskilinn EES-samningnum og vegna theirrar undanthagu geta islensk log bannath erlendum adilum ad eiga meira en 25% (eda 33% obeint) i islenskum utgerthum.
Frjálst flæði fjármagns ESB gerir ríkjum algjörlega ókleift að banna erlendum aðilum að kaupa hlutafé í innlendum fyrirtækjum.
ESB hvílir á fjórum frelsisréttindum (fjórfrelsið) — þar á meðal staðfesturétti og frjálsu flæði fjármagns.
Við ESB-aðild Íslands myndi íslenskur sjávarútvegur opnast fyrir staðfesturétti og frjálsu flæði fjármagns ESB.
Samkvæmt sameiginlegri sjávarútvegsstefnu ESB þyrftu erlend skip að fá leyfi samkvæmt íslenskum lögum og vera skráð undir íslenskan fána til þess að veiða í íslenskri lögsögu.
Samkvæmt regluverki ESB verður að skrá veiði, votta löndun, skrá sölu og halda rekjanleika til verslanna.
